validation.comparison_result: Q6 results match ground truth (1 row)

Q6.notes: Correct results. Same performance as iteration 1 (6ms). Optimizations applied (24 threads, false sharing fix, prefetching, AVX-512VL, 2x unrolling) but no speedup observed. This suggests we've hit a performance ceiling - likely memory bandwidth saturation or data already in page cache making I/O optimizations ineffective.

Q6.bottleneck: No improvement from iteration 1 despite multiple optimizations. Primary bottleneck is likely memory bandwidth saturation at 24 threads, or data in page cache making this a pure CPU-bound scan.
Q6.root_cause: Analysis of why 6ms persists: (1) Thread count only increased to 24 (not 64) - still leaving 62.5% of cores idle, (2) Memory bandwidth saturation - with 24 threads reading ~1.44GB of data (4 columns × 60M rows × 0.024 selectivity), we may be hitting the ~50-60GB/s memory bandwidth limit, (3) Data likely in page cache after first run - I/O optimizations have no effect, (4) Atomic contention on next_block counter becomes significant at 24 threads with 600 blocks (~25 blocks/thread), (5) Zone map pruning is highly effective - actual work is minimal, so optimizations to the SIMD kernel have limited impact, (6) SIMD improvements (AVX-512VL, unrolling) masked by bandwidth bottleneck.

analysis.overall: Q6 iteration 2 maintains correct results and 6ms execution time, same as iteration 1. Applied optimizations: increased thread count to 24, added false sharing prevention padding, improved prefetching, AVX-512VL int64 multiplication, and 2x SIMD loop unrolling. However, no performance improvement observed. This indicates we've reached a performance plateau where the bottleneck has shifted from CPU computation to memory bandwidth or other system-level constraints. At 6ms for 60M rows, we're already achieving exceptional throughput (~10M rows/ms), beating DuckDB by 3.2x. Further gains require more aggressive changes: using all 64 cores, reducing atomic contention, or investigating whether zone maps are over-pruning.

performance_optimizations.technique: Remove thread count cap and use all 64 cores
performance_optimizations.description: Lines 468-472 cap thread count at 24, leaving 40 cores (62.5%) idle. Remove the artificial cap: change `if (num_threads > 24) num_threads = 24;` to use full hardware_concurrency(). With zone map pruning reducing work to ~120-240 blocks (20-40% of 600 total), and each block taking ~25-50μs to process with SIMD, 64 threads can maintain adequate granularity. To reduce atomic contention on next_block counter, consider switching from single atomic counter to work-stealing queues or block-batch assignment (each thread fetches 2-4 blocks at a time instead of 1). Expected behavior: near-linear scaling from 24→64 threads until memory bandwidth ceiling (~50-60GB/s), which we haven't hit yet at 24 threads.
performance_optimizations.expected_improvement: 2-2.5x speedup (6ms → 2.4-3ms). Rationale: With zone maps pruning ~60-80% of blocks, remaining computation is highly parallel SIMD work with minimal synchronization. Memory bandwidth calculation: 60M rows × 0.024 selectivity × (4+4+4+8 bytes) × 24 threads = ~33.2GB/s, well below 50-60GB/s server bandwidth. Going to 64 threads increases to ~88GB/s, which will hit bandwidth ceiling, but we should still see ~2x improvement before saturation. Conservative estimate: 2-2.5x.

performance_optimizations.technique: Batch block assignment to reduce atomic contention
performance_optimizations.description: Line 408: `next_block.fetch_add(1, std::memory_order_relaxed)` causes cache line bouncing when 24-64 threads compete for the counter. With 600 blocks and 64 threads, each thread processes ~9 blocks, requiring 600 fetch_add operations total. Each fetch_add involves cache coherency traffic (~50-100 cycles). Solution: batch block assignment - each thread fetches B blocks at a time (e.g., B=4): `size_t block_start = next_block.fetch_add(B, std::memory_order_relaxed);` then processes blocks [block_start, block_start+B). This reduces atomic operations from 600 to 150, cutting synchronization overhead by 75%. Tradeoff: slight load imbalance at the end (last few threads may run out of work early), but with large block count (600), impact is minimal.
performance_optimizations.expected_improvement: 1.1-1.15x speedup at 64 threads (3ms → 2.6-2.7ms). Rationale: Atomic contention overhead at 64 threads is ~5-10% of total time. Reducing atomic ops by 75% saves ~4-8% overall. Conservative estimate: 1.1x.

performance_optimizations.technique: Use branch-free masking instead of movemask early exit
performance_optimizations.description: Lines 219-251 and 254-286 use movemask early exit optimization: check if any lanes match before loading extendedprice and computing aggregation. This is excellent for highly selective predicates. However, the movemask + conditional branch itself costs ~3-5 cycles, and mispredictions add ~15-20 cycles. For Q6 with 2.4% selectivity, ~2.4% of 8-element chunks will have at least one match, giving ~98% branch prediction accuracy (branch is almost always not taken). However, with SIMD processing 8 rows at a time, the probability that at least one row in a chunk matches is: 1 - (1-0.024)^8 = 17.5%, meaning the branch is taken 17.5% of the time. This creates moderate branch misprediction overhead (~17.5% × 20 cycles). Alternative: Remove the early exit branch and always load extendedprice + compute multiplication + apply mask. For 82.5% of chunks with no matches, we waste ~10 cycles on unnecessary loads/multiplies, but save ~3 cycles on movemask + branch. Net: ~7 cycle penalty for non-matching chunks, but eliminate ~20 cycle misprediction cost for 17.5% of chunks. Expected net gain: ~1.05x.
performance_optimizations.expected_improvement: 1.03-1.05x speedup (6ms → 5.7-5.8ms). Rationale: Early exit optimization trades computation for branches. With 17.5% match rate per 8-row chunk, branch misprediction overhead is ~3% of total time. Removing it saves ~2-3%. However, this optimization is speculative and may not help if branch predictor is highly accurate. Low priority, test carefully.

performance_optimizations.technique: Use huge pages (2MB) for column mmap to reduce TLB misses
performance_optimizations.description: Current code uses standard 4KB pages for mmap (line 80). With 60M rows × 20 bytes/row = ~1.2GB data, this requires ~307K page table entries. At 24 threads, each thread scans ~50MB, requiring ~12.8K page lookups. TLB (Translation Lookaside Buffer) on modern CPUs holds ~1536 entries (L1) + ~4096 entries (L2). With 12.8K pages per thread, TLB miss rate is high (~70-80%), causing ~10ns overhead per miss (walk page tables). With ~10M page accesses during scan, TLB misses add ~7-8ms overhead. Solution: Use huge pages (2MB) via `madvise(data, size, MADV_HUGEPAGE)` after mmap, reducing page count from 307K to ~600, fitting entirely in TLB. Requires kernel support (transparent huge pages enabled). Check with: `cat /sys/kernel/mm/transparent_hugepage/enabled`. If not available, fallback to standard pages.
performance_optimizations.expected_improvement: 1.2-1.3x speedup if TLB misses are significant (6ms → 4.6-5ms). Rationale: TLB miss overhead is ~10-15% of total time for large scans. Eliminating misses with huge pages saves this overhead. However, if data is already in L3 cache (hot data), TLB overhead is lower. This optimization is most effective for cold data or large working sets. Priority: test with huge pages enabled.

performance_optimizations.technique: Upgrade to full AVX-512 (512-bit) instead of AVX2 (256-bit)
performance_optimizations.description: Current code uses AVX2 (__m256i, 8×int32) with optional AVX-512VL for int64 multiplication. Hardware supports full AVX-512 (avx512f, avx512dq, avx512bw, avx512vl from lscpu flags). Upgrade to __m512i (16×int32) to double SIMD width: process 16 rows per iteration instead of 8. Changes: (1) Replace __m256i with __m512i, (2) Use _mm512_loadu_si512, _mm512_cmpgt_epi32_mask (AVX-512 uses mask registers, simpler than movemask), (3) Use _mm512_mask_mullo_epi64 for masked multiplication (eliminates separate AND operations), (4) Update loop to process 16 rows at a time. AVX-512 benefits: (a) 2x wider vectors, (b) mask registers simplify predicate logic, (c) masked operations eliminate AND masking. Caveat: AVX-512 may throttle CPU frequency on Skylake/Cascade Lake (~10-15% reduction). On Ice Lake+ servers, throttling is minimal (~5%). Check CPU generation and measure carefully.
performance_optimizations.expected_improvement: 1.3-1.6x speedup on SIMD portion if no frequency throttling (6ms → 3.8-4.6ms). With frequency throttling: 1.1-1.2x (6ms → 5-5.5ms). Rationale: Doubling vector width theoretically gives 2x, but (1) memory bandwidth limits scaling, (2) horizontal reduction overhead doubles, (3) frequency throttling on some CPUs. Net expected: 1.3-1.6x without throttling, 1.1-1.2x with throttling. Test on actual hardware to measure frequency impact: `perf stat -e cpu_core/event=0x3c/ ./q6 ...` to monitor cycles.

performance_optimizations.technique: Fuse zone map loading with block processing to reduce memory footprint
performance_optimizations.description: Lines 459-461 load all three zone maps (shipdate, discount, quantity) into memory, totaling ~600 blocks × 24 bytes/entry × 3 zone maps = ~43KB. This fits in L1 cache (32KB per core), but with 24-64 threads, total footprint is ~43KB × 64 = 2.7MB, exceeding L3 cache (44MB shared is fine, but per-thread L1 misses occur). Optimization: instead of passing zone maps to threads, have each thread load zone map entries on-demand as it processes blocks. This is cache-friendly since each block's zone map entry is accessed once, then discarded. However, benefit is marginal since 43KB is tiny. Alternative: if zone maps are stored sequentially on disk, use mmap for zone maps too (currently using open/read/mmap/munmap), avoiding allocation overhead.
performance_optimizations.expected_improvement: Minimal (<2% improvement, 6ms → 5.9ms). Zone maps are tiny (43KB) and accessed sequentially, so cache behavior is already excellent. This optimization reduces memory footprint but doesn't significantly impact performance. Low priority.

performance_optimizations.technique: Use direct I/O (O_DIRECT) to bypass page cache for large scans
performance_optimizations.description: Current code uses buffered I/O (standard mmap). For large scans (1.2GB), this populates page cache, potentially evicting useful data from other processes. With 188GB free memory, this isn't a concern, but for repeated queries or multi-tenant systems, direct I/O avoids cache pollution. However, direct I/O requires aligned buffers (512-byte or 4KB) and may reduce performance if kernel readahead is beneficial. For Q6 with zone map pruning creating sparse access patterns, direct I/O might help by avoiding caching of skipped blocks. Trade-off: direct I/O bypasses page cache entirely, so repeated queries don't benefit from caching. For single-run benchmarks, direct I/O may help; for repeated queries, buffered I/O is better.
performance_optimizations.expected_improvement: Neutral to slightly negative (-5% to +5%, 6ms → 5.7-6.3ms). Direct I/O trades page cache benefits for lower CPU overhead. For Q6 with zone map pruning, the effect is unpredictable. Test both approaches and choose based on workload (single-run vs repeated queries).

performance_optimizations.technique: Investigate zone map effectiveness - may be over-pruning
performance_optimizations.description: Q6 execution time is remarkably fast (6ms for 60M rows), suggesting either (1) zone maps are highly effective, pruning 80-90% of blocks, or (2) data is entirely in cache. To verify, add instrumentation: count how many blocks are skipped vs processed. Expected: ~360-480 blocks skipped (60-80%), ~120-240 blocks processed. If block skip rate is <50%, zone maps aren't helping much. If >90%, zone maps are excellent but leave little work for CPU optimization. Also check actual selectivity: 2.4% of 60M rows = ~1.44M rows should match. Verify this matches the aggregation input (count matching rows before aggregation). If zone maps are over-pruning (false negatives), results would be incorrect, but validation passed so this is unlikely.
performance_optimizations.expected_improvement: Diagnostic only - no direct performance improvement. Informs future optimization strategy: if zone maps prune <50%, improve zone map granularity or clustering. If >90%, focus on micro-optimizations (SIMD, threading) since total work is minimal.

summary: Q6 iteration 2 is correct and maintains 6ms execution time (same as iteration 1). Applied optimizations (24 threads, false sharing prevention, improved prefetching, AVX-512VL int64 multiplication, 2x SIMD unrolling) did not yield performance gains, indicating a performance plateau. Primary bottleneck: still only using 24 of 64 available cores (62.5% idle). Secondary bottleneck: likely memory bandwidth saturation or data in page cache making I/O optimizations ineffective. Recommended next steps: (1) Remove 24-thread cap to use all 64 cores (expected 2-2.5x gain → 2.4-3ms), (2) Implement batched block assignment to reduce atomic contention, (3) Consider huge pages for TLB miss reduction (1.2-1.3x gain), (4) Test full AVX-512 upgrade (1.3-1.6x gain if no frequency throttling). Combined potential: 6ms → 1.5-2ms, approaching hardware limits. Already beating DuckDB by 3.2x (6ms vs 19.3ms).
